10.title: Day 10 Assignment: Inheritance and Polymorphism
10.description: Deepen your understanding of inheritance chains, method overriding, and runtime polymorphism.

questions.title: super vs this in Inheritance
questions.question: What is the difference between `super` and `this` in Java? When must `super()` be called in a subclass constructor?
hints: this refers to the current object instance.
super refers to the parent class.
super() must be the first statement if called explicitly.
questions.solution:
**`this`**: Refers to the **current object**. Used to: access current class fields/methods, call another constructor in the same class (`this(...)`), or pass the current object as a parameter.

**`super`**: Refers to the **parent class**. Used to: call parent constructor (`super(...)`), access parent fields (`super.fieldName`), or call overridden parent methods (`super.methodName()`).

**When must `super()` be called?** Always implicitly, but explicitly when:
1. The parent class has no no-arg constructor (only parameterised constructors).
2. You need to pass specific arguments to the parent.

If you don't call `super()` explicitly, Java inserts `super()` (no-arg) automatically — which fails if no no-arg constructor exists in the parent.

questions.title: Dynamic Dispatch
hints: The declared type is Animal, but the actual object is Dog.
Java resolves overridden methods at runtime, not compile time.
This is the essence of polymorphism.
questions.solution:
**Output: Woof**

**Dynamic dispatch** means the JVM selects which overridden method to call based on the **actual runtime type** of the object, not the declared type of the reference.

- `Animal a = new Dog()`: reference type is `Animal`, actual object type is `Dog`.
- `a.speak()`: At compile time, Java checks that `Animal` has `speak()`. At **runtime**, the JVM sees the object is a `Dog`, so it calls `Dog.speak()` → `"Woof"`.

This is why polymorphism is powerful: you can write code against `Animal` and it works correctly for `Dog`, `Cat`, and any future subclasses — without changing the calling code.

questions.title: Avoiding Fragile Hierarchies
questions.question: A developer creates: `Employee → Manager → Director → CEO`. Each adds a salary multiplier. What problems can arise from deep inheritance hierarchies? How does the Liskov Substitution Principle relate?
hints: Deep hierarchies are fragile — changes propagate unexpectedly.
Liskov: subclasses must be usable wherever parent is expected.
Favor composition over inheritance for behaviour reuse.
questions.solution:
**Problems with deep inheritance:**
1. **Fragility**: A change in `Employee` can break `Manager`, `Director`, and `CEO`.
2. **Tight coupling**: All classes are tightly coupled to the parent chain.
3. **Overriding complexity**: Understanding what a method does requires tracing the entire chain.
4. **Testing burden**: Changes require testing all levels.

**Liskov Substitution Principle (LSP)**: Every subclass must be usable wherever its parent is expected, without breaking the program. Violated when a subclass restricts parent behaviour (e.g., a `CEO.setSalary()` that throws an exception, breaking code that calls `Employee.setSalary()`).

**Better approach — Composition:**
```java
class Employee {
    private SalaryCalculator calculator; // inject behaviour
    double getSalary() { return calculator.calculate(this); }
}
```
Composition is more flexible and doesn't create fragile hierarchies.

questions.title: Vehicle Hierarchy with Polymorphism
questions.question: Create a `Vehicle` base class with `fuelType()` and `maxSpeed()` methods. Create `Car` (petrol, 200 km/h), `ElectricCar` (electric, 250 km/h), and `Bicycle` (none, 30 km/h). Store them in a `Vehicle[]` and print each vehicle's type, fuel, and max speed.
hints: Override fuelType() and maxSpeed() in each subclass.
Store all in Vehicle[] array — polymorphism at work.
Use getClass().getSimpleName() for the class name.
questions.expectedOutput:
Car: fuel=petrol, maxSpeed=200 km/h
ElectricCar: fuel=electric, maxSpeed=250 km/h
Bicycle: fuel=none, maxSpeed=30 km/h

11.title: Day 11 Assignment: Abstract Classes and Interfaces
11.description: Understand when to use abstract classes vs interfaces and apply both effectively.

questions.title: Abstract Class vs Interface
questions.question: List at least 5 differences between abstract classes and interfaces in Java (post Java 8). When would you choose each?
hints: Abstract classes can have constructors and state; interfaces cannot (pre Java 9).
A class can implement multiple interfaces but extend only one class.
Think about IS-A vs CAN-DO relationships.
questions.solution:
| Feature | Abstract Class | Interface |
|---------|---------------|----------|
| Instance fields | Yes | No (only `public static final`) |
| Constructor | Yes | No |
| Multiple inheritance | No (single extend) | Yes (multiple implement) |
| Method types | Abstract + concrete | Abstract, default, static (Java 8+) |
| Access modifiers | Any | `public` by default |
| IS-A vs CAN-DO | IS-A relationship | CAN-DO capability |

**Choose Abstract Class when:**
- Sharing common code/state among closely related classes.
- IS-A relationship (a `Dog` IS-AN `Animal`).
- Need constructors or protected members.

**Choose Interface when:**
- Defining a capability/contract (`Comparable`, `Runnable`, `Serializable`).
- Multiple unrelated classes need the same behaviour.
- You want multiple inheritance of type.

questions.title: Default Methods and the Diamond Problem
questions.question: Java 8 added `default` methods to interfaces. If a class implements two interfaces that both have a `default void greet()`, what happens? How do you resolve the conflict?
hints: Compiler error: ambiguous default method.
You must override greet() in the implementing class.
Use InterfaceName.super.greet() to call a specific one.

questions.title: Payment System Design
questions.question: You are designing a payment system with: `CreditCard`, `DebitCard`, `PayPal`, `Crypto`. All can `processPayment(double amount)` and `getTransactionFee()`. Some (CreditCard, DebitCard) share card validation logic. How would you structure this using abstract classes and interfaces?
hints: Interface for the common contract (Payable).
Abstract class for shared card logic (CardPayment).
Non-card methods (PayPal, Crypto) implement interface only.

questions.title: Flyable and Swimmable
questions.question: Create interfaces `Flyable` (with `fly()`) and `Swimmable` (with `swim()`). Create classes: `Duck` (implements both), `Eagle` (only Flyable), `Fish` (only Swimmable). Print what each can do using instanceof checks.
hints: A class can implement multiple interfaces.
Use instanceof to check capabilities at runtime.
Consider adding a default method to each interface.
questions.expectedOutput:
Duck: Duck flies
Duck swims
Eagle: Eagle soars
Fish: Fish swims

12.title: Day 12 Assignment: Encapsulation and Immutability
12.description: Practice creating fully encapsulated and immutable value objects.

questions.title: What Makes a Class Immutable?
questions.question: List all the rules required to make a Java class truly immutable. Why does a mutable field (like a `List`) require special treatment?
hints: final class, final fields, no setters.
Defensive copying for mutable fields.
Return copies of mutable fields in getters.
questions.solution:
**Rules for immutability:**
1. Declare the class `final` (prevent subclassing that could break immutability).
2. All fields `private final`.
3. No setter methods.
4. Initialise all fields in the constructor.
5. **Defensive copy** mutable parameters in the constructor: `this.list = new ArrayList<>(input)`.
6. **Return copies** of mutable fields in getters: `return new ArrayList<>(this.list)`.

**Why mutable fields need special treatment:**
If you store a reference to an external `List` and return the same reference, callers can modify the list and change the 'immutable' object's state:
```java
List<String> external = new ArrayList<>();
MyImmutable obj = new MyImmutable(external);
external.add("surprise"); // obj's list is now modified!
```

Defensive copying breaks this shared-reference problem.

questions.title: Builder Pattern for Complex Objects
questions.question: When should you use the Builder pattern instead of a constructor? What problem does it solve?
hints: Think: 10+ constructor parameters, many optional.
Telescoping constructor anti-pattern.
Builder provides readable, step-by-step object creation.
questions.solution:
**When to use Builder:**
- Object has many parameters (4+), especially optional ones.
- Different combinations of parameters are valid.
- Want readable, self-documenting object creation.

**Problem: Telescoping constructors:**
```java
new Person("Alice", 30, null, null, null, "London", true);
// Which null is which? Error-prone!
```

**Builder solution:**
```java
Person p = new Person.Builder("Alice", 30)
    .city("London")
    .active(true)
    .build();
```

**Advantages:**
1. Named parameters (readable)
2. Optional fields have defaults
3. Validates in `build()` before creating the object
4. Thread-safe: builder builds the object, then it's immutable

questions.title: Defensive Copying Gone Wrong
hints: The constructor stores the caller's reference directly.
The getter returns the internal list directly.
Both require defensive copying.

questions.title: Immutable Money Class
questions.question: Create an immutable `Money` class with `amount` (BigDecimal) and `currency` (String). Add `add(Money other)` and `multiply(int factor)` methods that return NEW Money objects. Verify immutability in main.
hints: Fields: private final BigDecimal amount, String currency.
add() and multiply() return new Money(...).
Validate currency match in add().

13.title: Day 13 Assignment: Nested and Inner Classes
13.description: Understand the four kinds of nested classes and their practical use cases.

questions.title: Four Types of Nested Classes
questions.question: Name the four types of nested classes in Java. For each: can it access outer class members? Can it have static members?
hints: Static nested, inner (non-static), local, anonymous.
Only static nested can be used without an outer class instance.
Anonymous classes are typically used for short callback implementations.
questions.solution:
| Type | Outer Access | Has Static Members | Created With |
|------|-------------|-------------------|-------------|
| **Static nested** | Static members only | Yes | `new Outer.Nested()` |
| **Inner (non-static)** | All members (incl. private) | No | `outer.new Inner()` |
| **Local** | Effectively final locals | No | Inside a method |
| **Anonymous** | Effectively final locals | No | `new Interface() {...}` |

**Use cases:**
- **Static nested**: Helper/builder classes logically tied to outer (`Node` in `LinkedList`).
- **Inner**: Needs access to outer state (event listeners, iterators).
- **Local**: One-off class logic inside a method.
- **Anonymous**: One-time interface implementation (callbacks, Comparators pre-lambda).

questions.title: Memory Leak Risk with Inner Classes
questions.question: How can non-static inner classes cause memory leaks in Android/Java applications? What is the fix?
hints: Inner class holds an implicit reference to outer class.
If inner class lives longer than outer, outer cannot be GC'd.
Make it static or use WeakReference.

questions.title: Iterator with Inner Class
questions.question: Describe how Java's `ArrayList` implements `Iterator` as an inner class. Why is the inner class design appropriate here? What would happen if Iterator were a separate top-level class?
hints: ArrayList.Itr (inner class) accesses elementData[] directly.
Separate class would need a reference or package-private access.
Inner class is the cleanest encapsulation solution.
questions.solution:
**How ArrayList uses inner Iterator:**
`ArrayList` has a private inner class `Itr` that implements `Iterator<E>`. Because it's an inner class, it has direct access to `ArrayList`'s private fields: `elementData[]`, `size`, and `modCount`.

```java
private class Itr implements Iterator<E> {
    int cursor;       // next element index
    int lastRet = -1;
    int expectedModCount = modCount;

    public E next() {
        checkForComodification(); // uses outer modCount
        return (E) elementData[cursor++]; // accesses outer field
    }
}
```

**Why inner class is appropriate:**
- Direct private field access — no getters needed.
- Tightly coupled to ArrayList internals by design.
- Not useful outside ArrayList context.

**If it were separate top-level class:**
- Would need public/package-private access to ArrayList internals.
- Breaks encapsulation or creates awkward coupling.
- No logical reason for it to exist independently.

questions.title: Stack with Static Node
questions.question: Implement a generic `Stack<T>` class using a static nested `Node<T>` class as the underlying linked list node. Implement `push`, `pop`, `peek`, and `isEmpty`.
hints: Node<T> should be static nested: has T data and Node<T> next.
Stack maintains a reference to the top Node.
push creates a new Node pointing to current top.

14.title: Day 14 Assignment: Java Collections Framework
14.description: Master the Collections Framework hierarchy, choosing the right collection, and common operations.

questions.title: List vs Set vs Map
questions.question: Compare `List`, `Set`, and `Map` — their defining characteristics, ordering, and duplicate rules. Give one concrete implementation for each and a use case.
hints: List: ordered, allows duplicates. Set: no duplicates. Map: key-value pairs.
ArrayList, HashSet, HashMap are the most common.
Think about access patterns: indexed, membership, lookup by key.
questions.solution:
| Interface | Order | Duplicates | Null | Common Impl |
|-----------|-------|-----------|------|-------------|
| **List** | Insertion order | Yes | Yes | ArrayList, LinkedList |
| **Set** | No guaranteed (TreeSet: sorted) | No | One null (HashSet) | HashSet, TreeSet, LinkedHashSet |
| **Map** | No guaranteed (TreeMap: sorted) | Keys: No, Values: Yes | One null key (HashMap) | HashMap, TreeMap, LinkedHashMap |

**Use cases:**
- `ArrayList`: Shopping cart items (ordered, duplicates allowed).
- `HashSet`: Tracking visited URLs (fast membership check, no duplicates).
- `HashMap`: Cache (key=id, value=object), word frequency count.

questions.title: HashMap Internal Working
questions.question: How does `HashMap` work internally? Explain: hashing, buckets, collision handling, and load factor. What happens when load factor is exceeded?
hints: Key's hashCode() determines bucket index.
Collision: multiple keys in same bucket → linked list (Java 8+: red-black tree if > 8).
Default load factor 0.75 → resize at 75% capacity.
questions.solution:
**HashMap internals:**

1. **Hashing**: `key.hashCode()` is computed, then further hashed to reduce collisions. `bucket_index = hash & (capacity - 1)`.

2. **Buckets**: Internally an array of `Entry[]` (size defaults to 16). Each bucket holds key-value pairs.

3. **Collision handling**: Multiple keys can hash to the same bucket. Java uses:
   - **Linked list** (≤ 8 entries per bucket)
   - **Red-black tree** (> 8 entries, Java 8+) → O(log n) instead of O(n)

4. **Load factor** (default 0.75): When `size > capacity × 0.75`, HashMap **rehashes** — creates a new array (double size) and re-inserts all entries. This is O(n) but amortized O(1) for put.

5. **Equality check**: For `get(key)`, HashMap finds the bucket, then compares with `.equals()` to find the exact entry.

**Key point**: Always override both `hashCode()` and `equals()` consistently in custom key classes.

questions.title: Word Frequency Counter
questions.question: Design a solution to count word frequencies in a text. Which collection do you use? Handle case-insensitivity. Print the top 3 most frequent words. Input: "the quick brown fox jumps over the lazy dog the fox".
hints: HashMap<String, Integer> maps word to count.
Use getOrDefault to simplify counting.
Sort by value descending to find top 3.

questions.title: LRU Cache with LinkedHashMap
questions.question: Implement a simple LRU cache (Least Recently Used) with capacity 3 using `LinkedHashMap`. Override the `removeEldestEntry` method. Test by adding 4 entries and printing the cache.
hints: LinkedHashMap with accessOrder=true maintains access order.
Override removeEldestEntry to evict when size > capacity.
Use super constructor: new LinkedHashMap<>(capacity, 0.75f, true).

15.title: Day 15 Assignment: Iterators and Enhanced For Loop
15.description: Understand the Iterator pattern, Iterable contract, and fail-fast behaviour.

questions.title: Iterator vs Enhanced For vs ListIterator
questions.question: Compare Iterator, the enhanced for loop (for-each), and ListIterator. When would you use each?
hints: Enhanced for uses Iterator internally.
ListIterator supports bidirectional traversal and element modification.
Iterator.remove() is the safe way to remove during iteration.
questions.solution:
| Feature | Enhanced For | Iterator | ListIterator |
|---------|------------|----------|-------------|
| Remove during iteration | ❌ | ✅ (iterator.remove()) | ✅ |
| Add during iteration | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Bidirectional | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |
| Works on any Iterable | ✅ | ✅ | List only |
| Index access | ❌ | ❌ | ✅ |

**Use enhanced for**: Simple read-only iteration.
**Use Iterator**: Need to remove elements during iteration safely.
**Use ListIterator**: Need to add, set, or traverse backwards in a List.

questions.title: ConcurrentModificationException
questions.question: What is `ConcurrentModificationException`? When does it occur? How do you safely remove elements from a List while iterating?
hints: Triggered by structural modification during iteration (not via iterator).
modCount tracks structural changes.
Use iterator.remove() or iterate over a copy.

questions.title: Implementing Iterable
questions.question: You have a `NumberRange` class that represents [start, end]. You want to use it in a for-each loop: `for (int n : range)`. What interface must `NumberRange` implement and what method must it provide?
hints: Implement Iterable<Integer>.
Provide iterator() that returns an Iterator<Integer>.
Iterator needs hasNext() and next().

questions.title: Filter and Remove Evens
questions.question: Given a `List<Integer>` with values 1-10, use an `Iterator` to remove all even numbers from the list in-place. Print before and after.
hints: Get iterator from list.iterator().
Use iterator.remove() — never list.remove() inside the loop.
Check n % 2 == 0 to identify evens.

16.title: Day 16 Assignment: Comparable and Comparator
16.description: Master Java's two sorting contracts and apply them to custom object sorting.

questions.title: Comparable vs Comparator
questions.question: What is the difference between `Comparable` and `Comparator`? When would you use each? What do their `compareTo` and `compare` methods return?
hints: Comparable: natural ordering, implemented by the class itself.
Comparator: external ordering, any number of them.
Return negative, zero, or positive.
questions.solution:
**`Comparable<T>`** — defines the **natural ordering** of a class:
- Implemented **by the class itself**: `class Person implements Comparable<Person>`.
- One ordering per class.
- Used by `Collections.sort()`, `TreeMap`, etc. without explicit comparator.
- `compareTo(T other)`: returns negative (this < other), 0 (equal), positive (this > other).

**`Comparator<T>`** — defines an **external/custom ordering**:
- Implemented **separately** from the class.
- Multiple comparators possible (sort by name, by age, by salary...).
- `compare(T o1, T o2)`: same return convention.
- Passed to `Collections.sort(list, comparator)` or `list.sort(comparator)`.

**Choose Comparable** when there's one obvious 'natural' order (String alphabetical, Integer numeric).
**Choose Comparator** when you need multiple orderings or can't modify the class.

questions.title: Comparator Chaining
questions.question: Explain `Comparator.comparing()`, `thenComparing()`, and `reversed()`. Write the comparator chain to sort employees first by department (A-Z), then by salary (high to low).
hints: Comparator.comparing() takes a key extractor.
thenComparing() adds secondary sort.
reversed() inverts the order.

questions.title: Broken compareTo Contract
hints: What happens when prices are equal?
Three requirements: reflexive, antisymmetric, transitive.
compareTo(same object) must return 0.
questions.solution:
**Bug**: When `this.price == other.price`, the method returns -1 (instead of 0). This violates the compareTo contract: `x.compareTo(x)` must return 0 (reflexivity).

**Three contractual requirements:**
1. **Reflexivity**: `x.compareTo(x) == 0`
2. **Antisymmetry**: `sgn(x.compareTo(y)) == -sgn(y.compareTo(x))`
3. **Transitivity**: if `x < y` and `y < z`, then `x < z`

**Bonus**: Consistent with equals: `x.compareTo(y) == 0` should imply `x.equals(y)` (not mandatory but strongly recommended).

**Correct implementation:**
```java
@Override
public int compareTo(Product other) {
    return Double.compare(this.price, other.price);
}
```

`Double.compare()` handles all cases including NaN, negative zero, and equal values correctly.

questions.title: Student Sorting
questions.question: Create a `Student` class with `name`, `gpa`, and `year`. Sort a list of students: first by year ascending, then by GPA descending. Print the sorted list.
hints: Use Comparator.comparingInt(Student::getYear).thenComparing(...).reversed() carefully.
For GPA descending within same year: thenComparing with reversed.
Create test data with students in same year with different GPAs.
questions.expectedOutput:
Bob (Year 1, GPA 3.5)
Dave (Year 1, GPA 3.7)
Carol (Year 2, GPA 3.9)
Alice (Year 2, GPA 3.8)
Eve (Year 3, GPA 4.0)

17.title: Day 17 Assignment: File I/O and NIO.2
17.description: Practice reading and writing files using both classic java.io and modern NIO.2 APIs.

questions.title: java.io vs java.nio.file
questions.question: What are the main improvements that NIO.2 (java.nio.file) provides over the legacy java.io.File API? List at least 4 improvements.
hints: File.delete() returns boolean; Files.delete() throws IOException.
NIO.2 supports symbolic links, file attributes, directory streams.
Path is more feature-rich than File.
questions.solution:
**NIO.2 improvements over java.io.File:**

1. **Better error handling**: `Files.delete(path)` throws `IOException` with details. `File.delete()` silently returns false.

2. **Symbolic link support**: NIO.2 natively handles symlinks with `Files.isSymbolicLink()`, `Files.readSymbolicLink()`.

3. **File attributes**: Rich metadata access — creation time, permissions, owner — via `Files.readAttributes()`.

4. **Directory streams**: `Files.newDirectoryStream()` for efficient directory traversal without loading all entries into memory.

5. **File watching**: `WatchService` API for monitoring directory changes in real time.

6. **Walk file tree**: `Files.walk()` and `Files.walkFileTree()` for recursive operations.

7. **Atomic operations**: `Files.move()` can be atomic on same filesystem.

8. **Better I/O utilities**: `Files.readAllLines()`, `Files.writeString()`, `Files.copy()` — concise and efficient.

questions.title: try-with-resources for I/O
questions.question: Why is `try-with-resources` essential for file I/O? What interface must a resource implement to work with it? What happens if both the try block and the close() method throw exceptions?
hints: Resources must implement AutoCloseable.
close() is called automatically even if exception occurs.
If both try and close() throw, the close() exception is suppressed.

questions.title: Log File Analyser
questions.question: You have a large log file (potentially GBs) with lines in format: `[ERROR] message`. Design a solution that reads the file line by line (not all at once), collects all ERROR lines, and writes them to a separate `errors.log` file. What NIO.2 APIs would you use?
hints: Files.lines() returns a Stream<String> — lazy, line-by-line.
Don't use Files.readAllLines() — loads entire file into memory.
Files.newBufferedWriter() for writing output.

questions.title: Word Count Tool
questions.question: Write a program that reads a file path from args, counts: total lines, total words, and total characters. Print the results. Create a test file with 3 lines of text.
hints: Use Files.lines() to stream lines.
Split each line on whitespace to count words.
Use Files.readString() for total character count.

18.title: Day 18 Assignment: Enums and Records
18.description: Master Java enums as full classes and Records as immutable data carriers.

questions.title: Java Enums Are Classes
questions.question: What makes Java enums more powerful than constants in other languages? List at least 4 capabilities Java enums have beyond simple named constants.
hints: Enums can have fields, methods, and constructors.
Enums can implement interfaces.
Each constant can override methods differently.
questions.solution:
Java enums are **full classes** that extend `java.lang.Enum`:

1. **Fields and constructors**: `enum Planet { EARTH(5.97e24, 6.37e6); Planet(double mass, double radius) {...} }`

2. **Methods**: Can define methods like `getGravity()` on the enum.

3. **Abstract methods**: Each constant can provide its own implementation.
```java
enum Operation {
    ADD { @Override double apply(double a, double b) { return a + b; } },
    MUL { @Override double apply(double a, double b) { return a * b; } };
    abstract double apply(double a, double b);
}
```

4. **Interface implementation**: `enum Day implements Runnable { ... }`

5. **Built-in methods**: `values()`, `valueOf()`, `name()`, `ordinal()`.

6. **EnumSet/EnumMap**: Highly efficient collections for enum types.

7. **Switch expression support**: Full pattern matching in modern Java.

questions.title: Java Records
questions.question: What is a Java Record (introduced in Java 16)? What does `record Point(int x, int y) {}` automatically generate? When should you use records vs regular classes?
hints: Records auto-generate constructor, getters, equals, hashCode, toString.
Records are implicitly final and immutable.
Use records for data carriers/DTOs, not for classes with business logic.
questions.solution:
`record Point(int x, int y) {}` automatically generates:
1. **Canonical constructor**: `Point(int x, int y)` — validates and assigns components.
2. **Accessor methods** (not JavaBean-style): `p.x()`, `p.y()` (not `getX()`).
3. **`equals()`**: Compares all components by value.
4. **`hashCode()`**: Based on all components.
5. **`toString()`**: `Point[x=1, y=2]` format.

Records are:
- **Implicitly final** (can't be subclassed).
- **Immutable** (components are final).
- Can have additional methods, static fields, and compact constructors.

**Use records for:**
- Data Transfer Objects (DTOs)
- Value objects (Point, Money, DateRange)
- API response/request bodies
- Compound map keys

**Avoid records when:**
- Need mutable state
- Need inheritance hierarchy
- Have significant business logic

questions.title: Replacing Constants with Enum
hints: int constants: no type safety, any int can be passed.
Enum: type-safe, IDE auto-complete, switch support.
Add description field to enum constructor.

questions.title: HTTP Status Codes Enum
questions.question: Create an enum `HttpStatus` with at least 5 status codes (200, 201, 400, 404, 500). Each should have a numeric `code` and a `message`. Add an `isSuccess()` method (true for 2xx). Add a static `fromCode(int code)` factory method.
hints: Constructor: HttpStatus(int code, String message).
isSuccess(): return code >= 200 && code < 300.
fromCode: iterate values(), match by code, throw if not found.
questions.expectedOutput:
200 OK (success=true)
201 Created (success=true)
400 Bad Request (success=false)
404 Not Found (success=false)
500 Internal Server Error (success=false)
NOT_FOUND
